source.title: BSEE Accident Investigation Report - WD 80 D Structural Damage and Pollution Event

actions_taken: Deployment of contractors (Wild Well, Safezone, Seal-Tite) for monitoring and response
Installation of clamp on alignment pin plug on 24 January 2022
De-inventorying of tanks to prevent pollution hazards
Installation of containment pan beneath drip point
Flowback equipment deployment and pressure testing
Installation of Cast Iron Bridge Plug (CIBP) at 2,000 feet on 14 February 2022
Stripping and shearing operations to recover tubing
Perforation of 9-5/8 inch casing and cement placement in annulus
Installation of new wellhead and pressure testing to 3,000 psi on 29 March 2022
event.summary: On 10 January 2022, a well control and pollution event occurred at WD 80 D production platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The incident originated from the D-36 well where an alignment pin plug on the tubing head had been leaking gas since 11 January 2021. During a coiled tubing operation to set a plug and clean scale, the tool became stuck downhole and required mechanical extraction, which exacerbated tubing-to-casing communication. This caused the A Annulus pressure to rise from 50 psi to 2000 psi, resulting in excessive fluid returns to the wet oil tank. Approximately 107 gallons (2.5 barrels) of hydrocarbons were spilled. The platform was evacuated and remediation efforts continued through March 2022. Root causes included equipment failure (packer assembly design flaw, alignment pin corrosion due to inadequate coating maintenance), human performance error during coiled tubing operations, and organizational failures in communication and urgency to remediate.
recommendations: Operators should maintain proper coatings on wellhead equipment in corrosive saltwater environments
Well intervention plans should account for potential exacerbation of fluid returns and include adequate vessel and fluid handling capacity
Timely reporting of well anomalies to regulatory authorities is critical for prompt remediation
Scale and paraffin buildup should be managed proactively to enable safe well interventions
Management communication systems should ensure field observations are escalated and acted upon promptly

hazards.name: Scale and paraffin accumulation in wellbore

threats.name: Tubing-to-casing communication due to packer assembly failure
Alignment pin plug corrosion and failure
Mechanical damage during coiled tubing operations

consequences.name: Hydrocarbon release to atmosphere and ocean

controls.name: Wellhead coating maintenance program
controls.barrier_role: Prevent corrosion of alignment pin plug
controls.lod_basis: Preventive maintenance to protect equipment from corrosive environment

supporting_text: The failure of the alignment pin plug is likely due to corrosion. This corrosion was caused by a failure of the coating. The coating was not properly maintained by Fieldwood.
Fieldwood's failure to maintain proper coatings against the saltwater laden environment led to excessive corrosion and subsequent failure of the alignment pin plug.

controls.name: Daily annulus pressure bleed-down procedure
controls.barrier_role: Prevent pressure buildup and leakage from alignment pin plug
controls.lod_basis: Operational procedure to manage pressure and prevent uncontrolled release

supporting_text: Fieldwood, on at least a daily frequency, bled pressure from the A Annulus through the casing valve to the atmosphere, which effectively prevented the alignment pin plug from leaking.

With Hurricane Ida approaching in August 2021, Fieldwood determined that during a hurricane evacuation, personnel would not be able to bleed pressure from the casing daily. This would cause the alignment pin plug to leak hydrocarbons in an uncontrolled manner for the duration of the evacuation.

controls.name: Regulatory reporting of well anomalies to BSEE
controls.barrier_role: Enable regulatory oversight and prompt remediation of well issues
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory requirement to report gas leaks per NTL 2019-N05

supporting_text: Fieldwood failed to report the gas leak to the Bureau of Safety and Environmental Enforcement (BSEE) per the Notice to Lessees (NTL) No. 2019-N05.
The gas leak was not reported to BSEE until August of 2021 right before Hurricane IDA. If BSEE had knowledge of the leak, the agency would have prompted Fieldwood to remediate the issue.
BSEE has identified lack of communication within the Fieldwood organization to be a causal factor in allowing this wellhead failure to persist for several months without mitigation.

controls.name: Well intervention planning and risk assessment
controls.barrier_role: Identify and mitigate risks associated with coiled tubing operations
controls.lod_basis: Operational planning to account for potential consequences of well intervention

supporting_text: An additional contributing factor was a failure of the coiled tubing operations team to account the possibility that their actions could exacerbate the fluid returns from the tubing to through the casing. Had the workover plan accounted for this possibility, the crew could have included additional vessels and fluid handling capacity to account for this.

During this operation, the tool got stuck downhole and took several hours to be mechanically pulled, which caused stresses that may have caused larger communication from the production tubing to the A Annulus.

controls.name: Wet oil tank fluid handling and overflow prevention
controls.barrier_role: Prevent overflow and environmental release from wet oil tank
controls.lod_basis: Containment vessel to capture fluid returns during well operations

supporting_text: This vessel was not able to pump down levels because there were no viable pipelines to accept fluid from the platform.
The higher pressure in the production casing caused excessive fluid returns to the platform's wet oil tank.

Photos of the wet oil tank show that oil was pushed out of the vent at the top of the tank and sprayed upwards at least 7 feet leaving residue on the beams above the tank. At this point, the wet oil tank and sump tank reached capacity and continued to spill oil into the Gulf for several hours.

controls.name: Packer assembly design and installation
controls.lod_basis: Equipment design to isolate tubing from casing

supporting_text: Equipment Failure – Flawed equipment design or construction: Packer assembly and overshot did not work from the start when it was installed in 2013.
The most likely cause of tubing to casing communication is from the packer overshot assembly. Over time the communication became more pronounced.
In 2013, Dynamic cut the tubing at 3000 feet and set a packer and non-sealing overshot.

controls.name: Scale and paraffin removal operations
controls.barrier_role: Enable safe well intervention by removing scale buildup
controls.lod_basis: Proactive maintenance to prevent obstruction during well operations

supporting_text: The well has had a history of scale being encountered during well operations.
During a gauge run, QES could not get below 150 feet due to scale and paraffin build up in the tubing.
If scale had not continually been encountered during well operations, a plug could have likely been set deep enough to make a safe wellhead repair.
During the initial wireline operation encountered scale and could not get below 454 feet.

controls.name: Temporary hose routing to vent scrubber
controls.barrier_role: Capture and vent gas at controlled pressure during evacuation
controls.lod_basis: Temporary modification to prevent uncontrolled release during hurricane evacuation

supporting_text: Fieldwood obtained approval on 26 August 2021 to route a hose from the casing valve to the wet oil tank which connected to the vent scrubber's underwater vent. The purpose of this temporary modification was to allow liquids to be captured by platform vessels while gas could be vented to the atmosphere at a controlled pressure of 50 psi.

controls.name: Casing pressure monitoring and reporting
controls.barrier_role: Detect and track pressure buildup in annulus
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory requirement for ongoing monitoring post-remediation

supporting_text: BSEE required QNE and subsequently White Fleet Operating LLC (a designated agent for a predecessor lessee Hess) to monitor the casing pressures daily and report them to BSEE weekly.

The casing pressure reports show that although both A and B Annuli bleed to 0 psi, the 9-5/8" casing (A Annulus) still builds pressure at a rate of ~100 psi per day to the original pressure of ~2200 psi and the B Annulus build pressure to ~100 psi.

controls.name: Regulatory inspection and oversight
controls.barrier_role: Detect well anomalies and ensure compliance with regulations

supporting_text: BSEE conducted a Production Complete (annual) inspection on the facility on 16 July 2021. However, the leak was undetectable at the time of the inspection because the annulus was recently bled down.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
